fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon r\sim r'\colon A\to B and a witness h\colon r'\circ f \sim 1_{B} that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon r \circ f \sim 1_{B} that r is a retraction of f as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: H^*h x :\equiv H \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \cdot h x
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: As we have \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathsf {rx}_{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathord {\vartriangle }A}r\right \rparen \mathclose {} ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of -\circ f\sim 1_{A} over \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathord {\vartriangle }A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute are right-cloven over underlying maps.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Context

fr:title.text: Dependent path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Dependent path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We have seen in how to obtain principles for equational reasoning by imposing path object structures on types; in general, we will need an analogous tool for working with families of types B\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} given a path object \mathcal {A}. Such a tool must account for heterogeneous identifications in B, i.e. identifications between elements of different fibers of B; in this section, we explore three possible variations on the concept of “dependent path object”:

fr:ol.fr:li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
left-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
right-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased right- and left-cloven representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.

fr:title.text: Displayed path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B} over a reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for each x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} the component \mathcal {B} \mathopen {}\left \lbrack x\right \rbrack \mathclose {} is univalent. We define a displayed path object to be a univalent displayed reflexive graph.
We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj}\,\mathcal {A} for the (large) type of displayed path objects over \mathcal {A}.

fr:title.text: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed path object over \mathcal {A}. Then the total reflexive graph \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B} is a path object.
Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathcal {A} followed by univalence of \mathcal {B}.

fr:title.text: Left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. A left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} is given by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a pushforward operation assigning to each edge p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y a map p_* \colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} y\right \rvert \mathclose {} ;

a family \Phi _{\mathcal {B}} \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}\right \rparen \mathclose {} _*b \approx _{\mathcal {B} x} b witnessing the unit law that pushforward along the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} x is the identity function.

fr:title.text: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object and let \mathcal {B}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. A right cleaving of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} is defined by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a pullback operation assigning to each edge p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y a map p^* \colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} y\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} ;

a family \Phi _{\mathcal {B}} \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} b \approx _{\mathcal {B} x} \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}^*b witnessing the unit law that pullback along the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} x is the identity function.

fr:title.text: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord {\vartriangle }A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.
We next show how right-cloven and left-cloven families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base.

fr:title.text: Display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object. Any left-cloven family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over \mathcal {A} in which:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be p_*b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} b';

for x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} and b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} , the identity element \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {B}}{x}\colon b \approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}}b is given by the component \Phi _{\mathcal {B}} xb\colon \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}\right \rparen \mathclose {} _*b\approx _{\mathcal {B} x}{b} of the unit law of the given right cleaving of \mathcal {B}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each \mathcal {B} x.

fr:title.text: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object. Any right-cloven family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over \mathcal {A} in which:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} p^*b';

for x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} and b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} , the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {B}}^{x}{b}\colon b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}}b is given by the component \Phi _{\mathcal {B}}xb\colon b\approx _{\mathcal {B} x} \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}^*b of the unit law of the given cleaving of \mathcal {B}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of left cleavings (). Fixing a: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} , we must show that the reflexive graph structure on \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} with edges in b \approx _{\mathcal {B} a} \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{a}^*b' and reflexivity data for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab\colon b\approx _{\mathcal {B} a}\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{a}^*b is univalent.

It suffices to show that the type of singletons \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} b\approx _{\mathcal {B} a}\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{a}^*b' is a proposition. As each \mathcal {B} a is univalent, this is easily shown to be a retract of \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen b': \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} a\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} b\approx _{\mathcal {B} a}b', which is a proposition again by the univalence of \mathcal {B} a. Therefore, we conclude via .

fr:title.text: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a left-cloven family of path objects over a path object \mathcal {A}. We define the covariant sum of the family \mathcal {B}, written \mathchoice {\textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\mathcal {B} x}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to \mathcal {B} by . In particular, we have defined \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,u\right \rparen \mathclose {} \approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\mathcal {B} x}} \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,v\right \rparen \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} p_*u \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} v.

fr:title.text: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an right-cloven family of path objects over a path object \mathcal {A}. We define the contravariant sum of the family \mathcal {B}, written \mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathcal {A}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\mathcal {B} x}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to \mathcal {B} as in . In particular, we have defined \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,u\right \rparen \mathclose {} \approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx} \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,v\right \rparen \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} u \approx _{\mathcal {B} x} p^*v.

fr:title.text: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathcal {G} to \mathcal {G}' is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen x\approx _{\mathcal {G}}y\right \rparen \mathclose {} \cong \mathopen {}\left \lparen x\approx _{\mathcal {G}'}y\right \rparen \mathclose {} that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I\colon \mathopen {}\left \lparen \lambda {x}\mathpunct {.} H\,x\,x\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {G}}{x}\right \rparen \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} \sim \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {G}'}.

We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left \lparen -xx\right \rparen \mathclose {} is a left-cloven over the path object \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : \mathopen {}\left \lparen \approx _{\mathcal {G}}\right \rparen \mathclose {} \approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathsf {Type}} \mathopen {}\left \lparen \approx _{\mathcal {G}'}\right \rparen \mathclose {} of graphs and a reflexivity datum r\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} x\approx _{\mathcal {G}} x, we define H_*r x :\equiv H\,x\,x\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen rx\right \rparen \mathclose {} ; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathsf {Type}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathsf {Type}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathsf {Type}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathsf {Type}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left \lparen Exx\right \rparen \mathclose {} , in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

fr:title.text: Path object structure on fibers

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on fibers

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given f\colon A\to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}fx=b of f over b. The family \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left \lparen f-=b\right \rparen \mathclose {} \colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is right-cloven over \mathord {\vartriangle }A:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b\colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \phi ;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx=b\right \rparen \mathclose {} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\phi \right \rparen \mathclose {} \approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx=b\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,\psi \right \rparen \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p: x= y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p: x= y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p: x= y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p: x= y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \phi = \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \psi .

fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon s\sim s'\colon B\to A and a witness h\colon f\circ s' \sim 1_{B} that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon f \circ s \sim 1_{B} that s is a section of f as follows:

As we evidently have \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathsf {rx}_{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathord {\vartriangle }A}{s}\right \rparen \mathclose {} ^*h x = \mathsf {act}_{f}\mathsf {refl}\cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f\circ -\sim 1_{B} over the path object \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathord {\vartriangle }A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . In this sense, sectionhood witnesses are right-cloven over their underlying maps.

fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon r\sim r'\colon A\to B and a witness h\colon r'\circ f \sim 1_{B} that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon r \circ f \sim 1_{B} that r is a retraction of f as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: H^*h x :\equiv H \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \cdot h x
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: As we have \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathsf {rx}_{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathord {\vartriangle }A}r\right \rparen \mathclose {} ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of -\circ f\sim 1_{A} over \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:B\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathord {\vartriangle }A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute are right-cloven over underlying maps.

fr:title.text: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object and let \mathcal {B}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. A right cleaving of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} is defined by the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: a pullback operation assigning to each edge p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y a map p^* \colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} y\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} ;

a family \Phi _{\mathcal {B}} \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,b: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} b \approx _{\mathcal {B} x} \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}^*b witnessing the unit law that pullback along the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} x is the identity function.

fr:title.text: Dependent product of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Dependent product of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Homotopy

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f , g \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx be two dependent functions. A homotopy from f to g, writing H\colon f \sim g is given by a dependent function H\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}fx=gx. We shall write 1_{f}\colon f \sim f for the identity homotopy on f defined by 1_{f}x:\equiv \mathsf {refl}.

fr:title.text: Section-retraction pair

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Section-retraction pair
